coredata.dc:title: Utilization of cellulose-based carbon nanodots in sulfonated polysulfone based membrane for direct methanol fuel cell

coredata.dc:description: Sulfonated polysulfone (SPS) composite membranes consisting of cellulose-based carbon nanodots (CNDs) were successfully prepared as a proton exchange membrane (PEM) for direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) applications. The CNDs were prepared using the top-down hydrothermal method. The structure transformation from cellulose to CNDs was confirmed by the FTIR and XRD analysis. The particle size of CNDs was between 1 and 3 nm. The particles exhibited good optical properties. The incorporation of CND into matrix polymer increased the thermal stability of the membranes. Morphology analysis of the SPS/CND composite membranes revealed that CNDs had been successfully dispersed within the SPS polymer matrix. The cross-linking between SPS and CNDs enhanced the proton conductivity of the composite membranes compared to the pristine SPS membrane. The SPS/CND-1 (1 wt%) exhibited the highest selectivity with a proton conductivity of 35.5 mS.cm−1 and a methanol permeability of 3.50 × 10−6 cm2.s−1. The experimental results indicate that the incorporation of CNDs into the SPS membrane also decreased the amount of methanol that can pass through the membrane when used in DMFCs.

head.ce:title: Utilization of cellulose-based carbon nanodots in sulfonated polysulfone based membrane for direct methanol fuel cell

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfonated polysulfone (SPS) composite membranes consisting of cellulose-based carbon nanodots (CNDs) were successfully prepared as a proton exchange membrane (PEM) for direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) applications. The CNDs were prepared using the top-down hydrothermal method. The structure transformation from cellulose to CNDs was confirmed by the FTIR and XRD analysis. The particle size of CNDs was between 1 and 3 nm. The particles exhibited good optical properties. The incorporation of CND into matrix polymer increased the thermal stability of the membranes. Morphology analysis of the SPS/CND composite membranes revealed that CNDs had been successfully dispersed within the SPS polymer matrix. The cross-linking between SPS and CNDs enhanced the proton conductivity of the composite membranes compared to the pristine SPS membrane. The SPS/CND-1 (1 wt%) exhibited the highest selectivity with a proton conductivity of 35.5 mS.cm−1 and a methanol permeability of 3.50 × 10−6 cm2.s−1. The experimental results indicate that the incorporation of CNDs into the SPS membrane also decreased the amount of methanol that can pass through the membrane when used in DMFCs.
The SPS/CND membrane was fabricated for direct methanol fuel cell application.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, clean energy technologies are crucial for overcoming global pollution and fossil fuel depletion. One of the most promising among them is fuel cells. Fuel cells are electrochemical energy devices that convert a chemical reaction into electrical power without releasing pollutants (Martínez-Morlanes et al., 2015). Fuel cells cleanly and effectively transform chemical energy from hydrogen-rich fuels into electrical power and usable good-quality heat through an electrochemical process (Haile, 2003). A direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) is a type of fuel cell that uses diluted methanol as fuel (Permana et al., 2015). Methanol is used as the fuel source due to its high energy density, inexpensiveness, and ease of handling and storage (Kedang et al., 2022). The polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) is a substantial part of DMFCs. The principal purpose of a PEM in a DMFC system is to serve as a medium for transporting protons and reactants while also blocking the flow of electrons through the membrane. High proton conductivity (>10−2 S.cm−1) is a required property of a PEM to transport protons between the electrodes. In addition, a PEM must have superior chemical stability under operating conditions of fuel cells, be inexpensive to operate, and have a highly flexible membrane compatible with other cell components (Permana et al., 2020; Purwanto et al., 2016).

Most PEMs in fuel cells today are based on expensive perfluorinated membranes such as Nafion, which have high proton conductivity, excellent mechanical strength, and good stability. Unfortunately, Nafion can only operate at temperatures below 80 °C and relative humidity of 100 % to maintain proper proton conductivity (Kim and Kim, 2019). Moreover, Nafion has a high methanol permeation rate and a high production cost, which restricts its future commercialization.

Due to the aforementioned drawbacks, other polymers have been researched and developed as potential replacements that can exceed the performance of perfluorosulfonic acid membranes in fuel cells. There have been a growing number of research that have focused on the development of sulfonated hydrocarbon aromatic polymers-based PEM, such as sulfonated poly(ether ketone) (SPEEK) (An et al., 2016; Rico-Zavala et al., 2018; Rikukawa and Sanui, 2000; Song et al., 2016), sulfonated poly(p-phenylene) (Jo et al., 2016), sulfonated poly(arylene ether sulfone) (SPAES) (Kim et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2002), sulfonated poly(phenylene oxide) (SPPO) (Ge et al., 2015; Petreanu et al., 2012), sulfonated polybenzimidazole (SPBI) (Devrim et al., 2016; Singha et al., 2016), sulfonated polyethersulfone (SPES) (Assumma et al., 2014; Manea and Mulder, 2002; Muthumeenal et al., 2016), sulfonated polyphenylsulfone (SPPSU) (Kim et al., 2020; Mohamad Nor et al., 2020; Nor et al., 2021; Park et al., 2016), and sulfonated polysulfone (SPS) (Krathumkhet et al., 2018; Ozden et al., 2017; Rambabu et al., 2020) as the polymer backbone.

Among these aromatic polymers with non-perfluorinated backbone chains, SPS has gained the most attention due to its beneficial properties, including high chemical, mechanical, and thermo-oxidative stability, relatively low affinity for methanol, comparably high ionic conductivity, commercial availability, and low cost (Devrim et al., 2009). However, all of these desirable properties can only be achieved with a suitable degree of sulfonation (Tsai et al., 2007). Furthermore, there are two significant challenges in the use of SPS-based membranes, namely excessive swelling caused by the presence of hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups and decreasing proton conductivity caused by the degradation of sulfonic acid groups, particularly during high-temperature operation (Shahi, 2007).

Hybrid organic-inorganic nanocomposites have drawn a lot of attention due to their improved ability to withstand mechanical stresses while preserving features such as swelling capacity, water retention, and operating temperature. Several studies in this field have indicated that the addition of inorganic particles such as zirconium oxide (Rambabu et al., 2020), silicon oxide (Devrim et al., 2016; Padmavathi et al., 2012), zeolite (Krathumkhet et al., 2018), montmorillonite (MMT) (Kedang et al., 2022), zirconium hydrogen phosphate (ZrP) (Ozden et al., 2017), titanium oxide (TiO2) (Devrim et al., 2009; Martinez-Morlanes et al., 2021), carbon nanotube (CNT) (Altaf et al., 2020), and carbon nanodots (CNDs) (Mohamad Nor et al., 2020) to a wide range of polymers can enhance performance. In comparison to pure polymeric membranes, it is expected that these nanocomposite membranes can exhibit better properties.

The newest member of the nanomaterial family among these inorganic fillers is CNDs. CNDs are zero-dimensional nanomaterials that fall within the size range of less than 10 nm. Because of their great water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and ease of surface modification and functionalization, these new carbon nanomaterials have gained a lot of research interest (Dias et al., 2019; Zhi et al., 2018). The ability of the composite membrane to retain water can be further improved by nanomaterials that contain hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups (–OH, –COOH, etc.). These nanomaterials can also open up more channels for proton transport in the SPPSU polymer matrix. Nor et al. reported the use of crosslinked SPPSU membranes consisting of CNDs as a PEM for fuel cell application. The CNDs were obtained using the bottom-up hydrothermal method. The CNDs increased mechanical flexibility and contributed to increasing proton conductivity (Mohamad Nor et al., 2020). In addition, the fabricated membrane consisting of SPS polymer is classified as synthetic polymer that spent the lower price than Nafion 117 (USD 1400–2200/m2) (Avci et al., 2020). The utilization of biomass waste (Empty Fruit Bunches of palm oil, EFB) as source of CND is considerable due to the cellulose-based categorized as the most abundant waste in nature that easy to obtain (Ayinla et al., 2019). Reeb et al. (2014) reported literature value of purchase price of EFB biomass in average $12.02 per ton.

Based on our best knowledge, no reports of carbon nanodots being used as additives in SPS for DMFC applications have been reported. In addition, CNDs were obtained using top-down hydrothermal treatment. Therefore, this study aims to develop a novel cellulose-based carbon nanodot in a sulfonated polysulfone-based membrane for DMFC application. However, the study goals include creating CNDs and an SPS/CND composite membrane to obtain fundamental PEM characteristics, including proton conductivity, methanol permeability, and selectivity, that can be used as a PEM alternative for DMFC (Selyanchyn et al., 2020). It was critically analyzed how CND weight percentage loading affected the physical and chemical characteristics of the SPS membrane.

Empty fruit bunches (EFB) were used to synthesize cellulose. Polysulfone (PS) (average MW: 35 kDa, ≥99 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) ≥ 98 % were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) 98 % (PT Smart Lab. Indonesia), sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) 4.00–4.99 %, sodium chloride (NaCl) ≥ 99 %, acetic acid (CH3COOH) ≥ 99 %, urea (CO(NH2)2) 99 %, and N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc) 99.8 % were purchased from Merck. The grade of all chemicals are pure analysis.

A total of 50 g of EFBs were submerged in distilled water and heated at a temperature of 50 °C for 2 h and then filtered. Subsequently, EFBs were added with 4 % NaOH and stirred at 80 °C for 2 h. This process was carried out twice and dried in an oven. The dried pulp was added with 5 % NaOCl and a few drops of CH3COOH while being stirred at a temperature of 70 °C for 1 h. This process was also carried out twice in order to obtain cellulose (Marpongahtun et al., 2018).

CNDs were synthesized by means of the top-down hydrothermal method. Cellulose was added to 20 mL of distilled water containing CH4N2O (50 % (w/w) and then placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave. Subsequently, it was heated at a temperature of 160 °C for 12 h. The CND solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min. A reddish brown and foamy solid was formed (Fadllan et al., 2017).

Polysulfone (PS) was sulfonated by adding 5 g of PS to 5 M H2SO4 (1:10, w/w) and then refluxed at a temperature of 80 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, it was filtered and washed until neutral. The SPS was dried at 80 °C for 2 h (Blanco et al., 2002).

The SPS/CND membranes were synthesized using 5 g of SPS dissolved in 21.3 mL of DMAc and stirred for 16 h at room temperature. Subsequently, CND was added slowly and stirred for 4 h at room temperature. The dope solution was poured onto a flat glass plate and immersed in distilled water for 24 h. The obtained membranes were then dried for characterization (Altaf et al., 2020). Variations in membrane composition are shown in Table 1 , while Scheme 1 describes the possible reaction mechanism involved in the synthesis of the SPS/CND composite membranes.

The chemical interactions of the functional groups were investigated using FTIR (8400S Shimadzu). All the samples were disintegrated and blended thoroughly with KBr powder made for refinement. It was then formed into a pellet by a hydraulic press and placed in the sample holder. The wavenumbers used to scan the particles were in the range of 4000–400 cm−1.

X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Philips Xpert MPD, Cu Kα, λ = 1,5406 Å) was applied to determine the crystallinity peaks of EFB, cellulose, CNDs, and all of the membranes. The samples were placed on the pin tube holder and analyzed over a short range of 5°–50° with a step size of 0.017° and using Cu Kα radiation.
The size and shape of the CND were characterized using low-resolution transmission electron microscopy (LRTEM, TEM HT7700) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

The Malvern Particle Size Analyser was used to evaluate the particle size distribution at room temperature (25 °C), with a count rate of 46.7 kcps and a 1 min analysis period. The CND sample was dispersed in distilled water and subsequently sonicated for 20 min.
The absorption spectra of the CND solution were captured using a UV–Vis spectrometer (HITACHI Y-2009), carried out in deionized water, and monitored using a wavelength scan between 800 and 200 nm.

The Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (PERKIN ELMER LS 4) was used to record the fluorescence spectra. The process was carried out at normal temperature and pressure in deionized water. The fluorescence spectra were obtained using an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, a slit width of 10 nm, and wavelengths in the range of 800–200 nm.

The thermal properties of SPS and SPS/CND membranes were evaluated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TGA-50 Shimadzu), operating from 25 to 600 °C at a rate of 10 °C.min−1 under a nitrogen atmosphere.

All the samples' surface and cross-section characteristics were determined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (SEM, Zeiss-Evo Ma10). SEM was used to examine the membranes in low vacuum mode. The samples were sputtered with gold for around 120 min before analysis to ensure no charge on the surface. The sample was first freeze-fractured in liquid nitrogen and then sputtered with gold for cross-section characterization. The instrument is coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) detector for elemental analysis. The spatial resolution of the detected elements depends on the energy of the primary beam of electrons and the atomic number of the constituent elements, as well as the thickness of the sample material.

The tensile strength and elongation at the break of the composite membranes were used to calculate the mechanical properties. The thickness of the SPS, SPS/CND-1, SPS/CND-2, and SPS/CND-3 membrane samples were t = 0.12 mm, t = 0.10 mm, 0.13 mm, and 0.13 mm, respectively. The measurement was performed using a Tension Test Machine (Shimadzu, EZ-S) at room temperature with a constant crosshead speed of 2 mm.min−1.

The titration technique was used to calculate the ion exchange capacity (IEC). In order to convert H+ in the membrane samples into Na+, the samples were soaked in 1 M NaCl for 24 h. Subsequently, 0.01 M NaOH was used to titrate the mixture of membrane samples and NaCl solution. To indicate the end of the titration process, phenolphthalein (PP) was used. The volume and concentration of the NaOH solution, denoted by V NaOH and M NaOH, respectively, are both represented as values. The IEC was calculated using Eq. (1) as follows:

A mixture known as Fenton's reagent was made using a method similar to that described by Kedang et al. in order to determine the oxidative stability of the membranes (Kedang et al., 2022). The same amount of FeSO4.7H2O and 3 wt.% aqueous solution of H2O2 were combined in a glass beaker and shaken for 15 min to produce the Fenton's reagent. Samples taken from the membranes, each measuring 1 cm × 1 cm, were first weighed before being placed separately into glass beakers containing Fenton's reagent and stored at a temperature of 80 °C. The samples were periodically removed every 2 h from the beakers containing Fenton's reagent during the experiment in order to determine the weight loss at the appropriate intervals.

Weight difference measurements were carried out to assess water uptake (WU). The membranes were dried for 12 h at a temperature of 80 °C, and subsequently, their weight (W dry) and area (A dry) were noted. The membranes were then submerged in distilled water for 24 h at room temperature. The weight (W wet) and area (A wet) of the wet membranes were measured after the surface water had been carefully removed. Water uptake and swelling ratio are calculated using Eqs. Initial dehydration of the membranes was done at a temperature of 50 °C for 24 h. The membranes were then weighed. Subsequently, the membranes were submerged in methanol for 24 h. Afterward, a tissue paper was used to clean the membranes. The membranes were then weighed again. Methanol uptake was calculated using Eq. (5) as follows:

An Autolab PGSTAT204 system was used to measure the proton conductivity of the membranes at ambient temperature and 100 % relative humidity. Initially, the membranes were submerged in distilled water for 24 h. In addition, the membranes were delicately positioned in between the two electrodes of the fuel cell. The AC impedance spectra of the membranes were recorded within the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–1 MHz at oscillating voltages of 10–100 mV. Proton conductivity was calculated using Eq. (6) as follows:
(6)
σ =
L RxA
where σ is the proton conductivity (S.cm−1), R is the membrane resistance (Ω), and L and A are the thickness (cm) and surface area (cm2), respectively.

Additionally, the methanol crossover of the membranes was assessed using two compartments (A and B), each of which contained 100 mL of pure water and 100 mL of a 2 M methanol solution. The membrane was positioned in the space between the two chambers. Each compartment was additionally stirred continuously for 1 h. Every 15 min, 10 ml of the solution from compartment A was poured into a pycnometer and subsequently weighed. The same process was performed for the other membranes. Permeability was calculated using Eq. (7) as follows:

where P is methanol permeability (cm2.s−1), A is the surface area (cm2), d is the membrane thickness (cm), V a is the water volume at compartment A (cm3), C b is the methanol concentration in compartment B (mol.L−1), ΔC b/Δt is the difference of methanol concentration in compartment B as time function (mol.L−1.s−1).

The selectivity was measured through the ratio between the conductivity and methanol permeability. The selectivity was calculated using Eq. (8) as follows:

where β is the selectivity (S cm3 s), σ is the conductivity (S cm−1), and P is methanol permeability (cm2 s−1).

In general, there are two methods that are used to synthesize CNDs, namely the top-down method and the bottom-up method (Alas et al., 2020). In this study, the top-down hydrothermal method was used for the synthesis of CNDs because it is simple and inexpensive and because the cellulose used in the experiment was derived from biomass waste. EFBs are used as cellulose precursors because it is known to have the highest cellulose and hemicellulose content among palm oil wastes (Ayinla et al., 2019). The process of isolating cellulose from EFBs was carried out by adding alkali followed by a bleaching process to remove lignin, hemicellulose, wax, pectin, and impurities (Marwah et al., 2014; Ouajai and Shanks, 2005).

Fig. 1 (a) shows the IR spectra of EFBs. The absorption band at 1541 cm−1 is due to CC aromatic vibration from the presence of lignin (Rosa et al., 2012). The absorption band at 1740 cm−1 indicates CO stretching from hemicelluloses (Nuruddin et al., 2011). These two peaks were absent after the hydrolysis process indicating that lignin and hemicellulose were successfully removed from the EFBs.

The broad absorption band at 3400–3500 cm−1 is due to the stretching of –OH groups, and the absorption at 2900 cm−1 is related to CH2 groups (Jahan et al., 2011). The absorption band for the C–O strain within the cellulose ring appears at 1319.31 cm−1. The absorption band at 1163 cm−1 corresponds to C–O–C stretching, while the peak at 896 cm−1 is associated to the C–H rock vibration of cellulose (anomeric vibration, specific for β-glucosides) (Fahma et al., 2010). The FTIR spectra of cellulose is shown in Fig. 1(b).

Fig. 2 shows the XRD spectra of EFBs and cellulose. The change in crystallinity and structure of the EFBs into cellulose was confirmed by XRD. The peaks in the XRD spectra of EFBs at around 2θ = 22.6° is a typical characteristic of amorphous lignocellulosic. The main characteristic peaks of crystalline cellulose that appear at 2θ = 15.0°; 16.4°; 22.9° and 34.3° correspond to the (110), (110), (200), and (004) lattice crystallographic planes of cellulose type I, respectively (Holilah et al., 2022). (JCPDS-ICDD: 50–2241). The FTIR and XRD data confirmed that cellulose was successfully isolated from EFB.

In this research, CNDs were synthesized by means of hydrothermal treatment of cellulose from EFBs. CNDs are known as carbonaceous materials that are comprised of around 70–90 % carbon and oxygen. CNDs are also comprised of minor elements such as hydrogen, nitrogen, and other elements (Manioudakis et al., 2019). The proposed chemical structure of CNDs can be seen in Scheme 1, and the FTIR spectra of CNDs can be seen in Fig. 3 (a). The broad peaks at 3300 cm−1 and 3435 cm−1 indicate symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching of –OH and N–H, respectively. The peaks at around 1000–1200 cm−1 are attributed to O–H bending, indicating the abundance of hydroxyl groups. The characteristic peaks between 2800 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1 indicate C–H stretching. In addition, the presence of the amine group was further verified by the presence of CO stretching vibration in the primary/tertiary amides at around 1650 cm−1. The strong absorption obtained at 1534 cm−1 corresponds to N–H asymmetric vibration, and the peak at 1429 cm−1 is attributed to C–N bending vibration. The presence of various functional groups in CNDs that cause CNDs to become highly hydrophilic is indicated by the FTIR spectra (Mohamad Nor et al., 2020). The FTIR data is consistent with that of other studies that have also carried out cellulose-based synthesis of CNDs (Abinaya et al., 2021; Quang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2020).

The phase of the obtained CNDs was identified by the XRD pattern shown in Fig. 3(b). The broad peak at 2θ = 24° is attributed to the (002) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon. Interlayer spacing determined from the (002) peak corresponds to d 002 = 0.34 nm, which is similar to those of previously documented CNDs (Mohapatra et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2013). The sharp peak centered at 2θ = 44° corresponding to the (101) plane originated due to the in-plane diffraction of graphene-like structured CNDs (Jones et al., 2017). The XRD pattern reveals the presence of an amorphous carbon frame in cellulose. The existence of functional groups in abundance may have caused the amorphous structure.

As previously mentioned, CNDs are a new class of carbonaceous nanomaterials with a size of below 10 nm. In this study, the hydrothermal reaction was conducted for 12 h as it produced stable CNDs with a size of less than 10 nm. The TEM image of as-prepared CNDs is shown in Fig. 3(c). It is revealed that CNDs are monodispersed particles in a spherical form with a diameter of less than 10 nm. The corresponding size distribution histogram is plotted in the insert of Fig. 3(c), from which the majority particle size distribution between 1.3 and 1.5 nm is observed, and the average diameter is calculated to be 1.4 nm. These results are in line with the study conducted by Xu et al. that reported the synthesis of CNDs from grape peel by means of hydrothermal treatment at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 h (Xu et al., 2015).

The CNDs exhibited excellent optical properties. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CNDs is shown in Fig. 3(d). Two peaks are exhibited at 270 nm and 300 nm, which are attributed to CC bonds (ππ* transition) and CO bonds (n = π*), respectively (Zhao et al., 2018, 2020; Zhu et al., 2015). The formation of CNDs was confirmed by their color, which was brownish yellow under daylight and bright luminescent blue under UV illumination (365 nm). These results are consistent with the study conducted by Amjad et al. that reported the synthesis of CNDs from bovine gelatine by means of hydrothermal treatment (Amjad et al., 2019). In the study conducted by Amjad et al., an absorbance peak at 268 nm can be exhibited in the UV–Vis spectrum of the CNDs due to π - π* transitions of CC (aromatic carbon) bonds. Furthermore, a broad shoulder starting from 300 nm can also be exhibited in the UV–Vis spectrum of the CNDs due to n-π* of CO (carbonyl) bonds (Amjad et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR and XRD of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, PS is a thermoplastic material and a hydrophobic polymer that has an aromatic backbone (Liu et al., 2015). PS has low ionic conductivity, and in order to enhance its ionic conductivity, sulfonic acid groups (SO3H) are anchored at its backbone by an electrophilic substitution reaction, termed as sulfonation (Smitha et al., 2008). Fig. 4 (a) shows the FTIR spectra of PS, and Fig. 4(b–e) shows the FTIR spectra of the different SPS/CND composite membranes. The sulfonation process was successfully conducted, resulting in new peaks at 1680 cm−1 that are associated with the stretching vibration of benzene rings and absorbance bands at 1037 cm−1 that correspond to OSO from sulfonate groups in all the composite membranes (Altaf et al., 2020).

A new peak at 1534 cm−1 can be seen in the FTIR spectra of the composite membranes. This proves the presence of CNDs inside the SPS polymer matrix. This phenomenon indicates the occurrence of hydrogen bonding between the CNDs and SPS polymer matrix through a crosslinking process (Mohamad Nor et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 4 (b–e), the O–H stretching band was detected at wavenumbers between 3200 cm−1 and 3600 cm−1, in which the vibration was assigned from O–H vibration from the sulfonic acid group and CNDs. The intensity of the O–H stretching increased as the CNDs loading increased. This may be due to the hydrophilic behavior of CNDs and sulfonate groups that did not take part in the crosslinking process and, therefore, moved freely and reacted with water. This caused an increase in the water uptake of all the composite membranes, which is consistent with other studies (Mao et al., 2015; Mohamad Nor et al., 2020). The crosslinking reaction occurred mainly between the sulfonic acid group in the SPS and the amine group in the CNDs. Scheme 1 shows the possible crosslinking mechanism of the SPS/CND membrane. CNDs can easily form chemical bonds with other compounds due to their unique structure as a result of their small size and rich surface functional groups (Zhao and Chung, 2018).

The XRD pattern of the SPS and SPS/CND membranes are shown in Fig. 5 . The pristine SPS membrane showed a broad peak with a maximum intense peak at 2θ = 17.5°, which reflects the low crystallinity of this material and is consistent with other studies (Martinez-Morlanes et al., 2021; Smitha et al., 2008). After cross-linking with CNDs, the maximum intense peak shifted to 2θ = 18.5° and became narrower than the SPS membrane, indicating that the SPS/CND membranes are more amorphous. However, the positions of the peak related to the two components of the composite were more or less similar to the individual peaks of SPS and CND, indicating that the orientation of the SPS chains or the structure of CNDs did not undergo any significant changes during the synthesis process.

The decomposition patterns of SPS and SPS/CND composite membranes in a wide temperature range were investigated by TGA analysis, as presented in Fig. 6 . There are three steps to weight loss of SPS. In the first step, the weight loss between 30 °C and 180 °C is the loss of water and residual solvent. The second step loss, between 180 and 400 °C, can be attributed to the splitting-off of the sulfonic groups. The third thermal degradation is the decomposition of the polysulfone backbone, occurring at around 500 °C (Krathumkhet et al., 2018). The TGA curve of SPS/CND membranes shows four weight loss stages at 30–120 °C, 120–240 °C, 240–400 °C, and 500–550 °C, respectively. The first weight loss can be attributed to the loss of moisture present in the sulfonated polymer (Ryu et al., 2022). The second weight loss peak is believed to be due to the splitting of sulfonic acid groups (-SO3H groups) (Ozden et al., 2017). The third weight loss is attributed to the decomposition of CND, and the thermal degradation of the polysulfone backbone occurred at 500 °C.

The compatibility of the polymer and inorganic fillers, as well as the dispersion state of the inorganic fillers in the polymer matrix, significantly impact the morphological, physicochemical, mechanical, and thermal properties of the composite membranes (Ozden et al., 2017). Fig. 7 (a) shows the surface morphology of SPS(i) and SPS/CND(i) membranes. All membranes exhibited a smooth morphology. Surface smoothness confirmed the homogenous distribution of the CNDs in the membranes. Additionally, all the membranes exhibited a dense and non-porous construction with a smooth surface in which cracks or dimples are absent. The cross-section images of SPS(ii) and SPS/CND(ii) show that the induction of CND into the SPS polymer matrix has increased the pore size, and the composite membrane seems more porous than the base membrane. This might be the result of the fast change of solvent and non-solvent states during the phase inversion process. The phase inversion results in a membrane that is dense on the surface while the bottom is porous.

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy attached with SEM was used for elemental analysis of prepared SPS and composite membranes. Fig. 7(b) shows that the composite membrane exhibits peaks for carbon, oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen referring to their binding energies. The proportion of carbon atoms is about 70 %. The presence of nitrogen atoms in the EDX spectrum indicates that CND has been successfully incorporated into the SPS matrix, while the presence of oxygen and sulfur are due to the – SO3H groups of SPS. The element overlay of the spectrum shows that all elements are evenly distributed in the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties and oxidation stability of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties of the SPS and SPS/CND membranes were analyzed by means of the tensile test at room temperature. Fig. 8 shows the tensile strength and elongation at the break of the SPS and SPS/CND membranes. The value of tensile strength was between 27.65 MPa and 58.90 MPa, while the elongation at the break of the membranes was in the range of 51.9–52.74 %. The incorporation of CNDs inside the SPS polymer matrix increased the mechanical strength of the composite membranes, as shown in Fig. 8(a). The crosslinking between the polymer matrix and CNDs restricts the motion of the polymer chains, resulting in an increase in the tensile strength of the composite membranes. The tensile strength of the composite membranes was higher than that of the pristine SPS membrane. The SPS/CND-1 membrane obtained the highest tensile strength. These results are in line with the study conducted by Nor et al. that reported crosslinked membranes as a result of crosslinking between SPPSU and CNDs using the bottom-up method (Mohamad Nor et al., 2020). The cross-linking between the sulfonic group of SPS and the amine group of CNDs may result in a more compact and rigid membrane structure, hence the incorporation of CNDs into the membrane (Rhim et al., 2004). Unfortunately, increasing CNDs loading into the polymer matrix causes the tensile strength to decrease. This is because not all of the CNDs were involved in the crosslinking with the polymer matrix, so they did not contribute to the membrane's mechanical properties (Wang et al., 2017).

As shown in Fig. 8(b), the elongation at break of the SPS and SPS/CND membranes gradually increased as the CNDs loading increased. The composite membrane with a CNDs loading of 3 wt% had the highest membrane flexibility of 52.74 %. Good flexibility leads to a good interface between the membrane and electrodes and also prevents membrane cracking. A membrane must have high flexibility in order for it to have low thickness (Smitha et al., 2005).

The oxidative stability of the SPS/CND composite membranes were investigated at a temperature of 80 °C for 8 h for evaluation of their stability against severe attacks from radical species (e.g., HOO• and HO•) that exist in the Fenton's reagent. The samples from the SPS/CND composite membranes were periodically weighed and visually examined under a magnifying lens for any physical degradation during the oxidative stability analysis. The weight loss of each membrane was recorded every 2 h by subtracting the membrane's initial weight from the membrane's weight at every interval. The weight loss of the membranes at each interval is shown in Table 2 . The resistance of the membranes towards chemical degradation did not significantly change, despite the fact that the sample of the SPS/CND-1 membrane had a slightly higher oxidative stability than that of other membranes. Furthermore, no physical signs of deterioration were found after 8 h of analysis, demonstrating that all the examined membranes are capable of retaining their oxidative stability under extreme oxidizing conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water uptake, methanol uptake, and swelling ratio of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Measurements of water uptake (WU), methanol uptake (MU), and swelling ratio (SR) of the membranes were carried out to examine the effect of CNDs loading on membrane crosslinking. The WU, MU, and SR values of the membranes are listed in Table 3 . It can be seen in Table 3 that as the concentration of CNDs in the polymer matrix increased, the WU also increased. This was caused by the C–OH functional group in the CNDs matrix, which also contributed to enhancing the ability of the crosslinked membrane to retain water (Javed et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2014). Furthermore, it was found that the WU value of the pristine SPS membrane and the crosslinked membranes is higher than that of Nafion 117. A higher WU value is beneficial for a PEM as it facilitates proton hopping, which enables proton diffusion (Priyangga et al., 2021). Water is transported by diffusion, electro-osmotic effect (the migration of hydrogen ions), pressure gradients, and electrochemical reactions (Rikukawa and Sanui, 2000). Proton transport is inhibited by insufficient hydration, which leads to poor proton conductivity. However, an increase in WU results in a decrease in the intermolecular force between polymer chains, which in turn reduces mechanical strength (Kaffel, 2017; Kumari et al., 2018). It can be seen in Fig. 8 that the tensile strength of the membranes decreased as the WU increased. Therefore, a membrane must have an adequate water retention ability and also an adequate tensile strength. Furthermore, an increase in WU of the membranes did not result in a significant increase in SR. The SR of the pristine SPS and crosslinked membranes is lower than that of Nafion 117, as shown in Table 3. The cross-linking process enhances the mechanical properties of membranes and reduces the hydrophilic character of SPS, which decreases membrane swelling (Papadimitriou et al., 2011).

MU is a parameter that needs to be measured because it indicates the affinity of membranes towards methanol. A high MU causes an increase in fuel loss, which in turn decreases the fuel efficiency of the DMFC (Purwanto et al., 2016). It can be seen in Table 3 that the incorporation of CNDs decreased the MU of the composite membranes. The pristine SPS membrane exhibited the highest MU compared to all the composite membranes. Unfortunately, a further increase in CNDs loading into the polymer matrix led to an increase in methanol uptake. This phenomenon is caused by the increasing hydrophilicity of the composite membranes. In addition, high CNDs loading causes the membrane to become more porous and, as a result, makes it easier for methanol molecules to penetrate through the membrane. This feature was confirmed by the methanol permeability of the composite membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IEC, proton conductivity, methanol permeability, and selectivity of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The IEC, proton conductivity, methanol permeability, and selectivity of the membranes are listed in Table 4 . IEC can be interpreted as the ability of an insoluble functional group within the membrane to endure the displacement of ions, which are weakly attached to its structure by the opposite charged ions that exist in the adjacent solution (Vinodh and Sangeetha, 2013). Furthermore, IEC quantifies the total number of active sites or functional groups responsible for ion exchange in a PEM. It is assumed that increasing the amount of sulfonic acid groups in the polymer matrix will result in a higher IEC value of the membranes (Rhim et al., 2004). The IEC and DS value of the SPS polymer that was used to fabricate SPS/CND membranes were 2.18 and 80.8 %, respectively. This high DS of the SPS polymer was later correlated to the high peak intensity found in the FTIR spectra of the SPS/CND membranes and decreased with increasing CND loading. The IEC value of the membranes were within the range of 1.45 mmol.g−1 to 2.18 mmol.g−1. It can be seen in Table 4 that as the CNDs loading increased, the IEC value of the composite membrane decreased. The quantity of sulfonyl groups in the polymer chain generally affects the IEC value. In this study, the amount of SPS decreased as the concentration of CNDs was increased. This resulted in a decrease in the number of sulfonic acid groups at a certain weight of the polymer, which decreased the IEC as the weight concentration of CNDs was increased. These results are consistent to that of other studies (Li et al., 2009; Mohamad Nor et al., 2020).

Proton conductivity is the most important parameter of a PEM in fuel cell applications. Table 4 shows the proton conductivity of the pristine SPS membrane and the composite SPS/CND membranes. It can be seen in Table 4 that as the CNDs loading into the polymer matrix increased, the proton conductivity of the composite membrane increased. The sulfonate groups that are present in the composite membranes act as an active site to transfer protons within the membrane. There are several membrane properties that influence the efficiency of proton conductivity, namely water uptake, number of available acid groups, and their dissociation capability (Brijmohan et al., 2005). The presence of water within a PEM positively influences proton conductivity (Schechter and Savinell, 2002; Xiao et al., 2005). The proton conductivity values of the composite membranes were within the range of 35.5–38.8 mS.cm−1. Based on the obtained data, it can be seen that CNDs enhanced the transport of protons across the composite membranes. The SPS/CND-3 membrane exhibited the highest proton conductivity compared to the other samples. However, it is important to note that even though the SPS/CND-3 membrane had the highest proton conductivity, it showed deterioration in its mechanical stability compared to the SPS/CND-1 membrane. The incorporation of CNDs provided a better conductive pathway for proton transport within the SPS polymer matrix.

Methanol crossover can result in low fuel efficiency and decreased cathode activity due to the mixed potential effect and mass transfer effect at the cathode, so a lower methanol permeability is desirable (Wang et al., 2009). In this study, the methanol permeability was measured using a glass cell composed of two identical compartments that serve as a diffusion cell. It can be seen in Table 4 that the pristine SPS membrane had a significantly higher methanol permeability than the fabricated composite membranes. Compared to the pristine SPS membrane, the SPS/CND-1 composite membrane had a significantly lower methanol permeability. The methanol permeability increased as the CNDs loading into the polymer matrix was increased. In the pristine SPS membrane, the SO3H groups facilitate methanol permeation via hydrogen bonding. In the composite membrane with a CNDs loading of 1 wt%, the content of sulfonic acid decreased, and as a result, methanol permeation also decreased. In addition, the interaction between CNDs (-NH) and SO3H groups of SPS suppressed the formation of methanol permeation paths. Furthermore, an increase in the amount of CNDs results in poor dispersion of CNDs due to agglomeration, and as a result, the interaction between CNDs and the matrix was not sufficient to inhibit methanol permeation. In the previous study, Krathumkhet et al. (2018) reported that a high loading of zeolite ZSM-5 into the SPS matrix increased the composite membrane's methanol permeability. This was also the case in the study conducted by Padmavathi et al. (2012), in which a high loading of silica into the SPS matrix also resulted in an increase in methanol permeability of the composite membrane.

It is ideal for PEMs to have low methanol permeability and high proton conductivity. Furthermore, a higher selectivity can enhance the performance of the membrane. Proton conductivity and methanol permeability both contribute to selectivity. The selectivity was measured through the ratio between the conductivity and methanol permeability, as shown in Table 4. The addition of CND as filler into SPS membrane increased the selectivity of the composite membrane. The increasing of selectivity indirectly obtained by high proton conductivity and low methanol permeability. The highest selectivity was achieved by the SPS/CND-1 composite membrane at 1.01 × 104 S.s.cm−3 due to high proton conductivity and low methanol crossover as well, followed by the SPS/CND-2 membrane and the SPS/CND-3 membrane. The pristine SPS membrane obtained the lowest selectivity. Scheme 2 shows the plausible proton transport behaviour for SPS/CND membrane.

SPS composite membranes consisting of cellulose-based CNDs with high proton conductivity and low methanol permeability were successfully prepared using the phase inversion method. In this study, cellulose-based CNDs were prepared using the top-down hydrothermal method. Analysis of particle size and TEM image revealed that the particle size of the CNDs was within the range of 1–3 nm. The FTIR and XRD analysis showed the transformation of cellulose into CNDs. UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy revealed the optical characteristics of the CNDs. Morphological studies confirmed the dispersion of CNDs within the SPS matrix polymer. The induction of 1 wt.% CND into SPS polymer matrix enhanced the WU up to 45.6 % which is sufficient for fuel cell application. The tensile strength and elongation break also enhanced up to 58.90 MPa and 52.27 %, respectively. The composite membrane showed increasing the proton conductivity (35.5 mS.cm−1) compared to the base SPS membrane (34.6 mS.cm−1). The composite membrane showed excellent reduction in methanol permeability (3.50 × 10−6 cm2/s) for SPS/CND-1 compared to the base SPS membrane (7.64 × 10−6 cm2/s) and Nafion 117. Therefore, the SPS/CND-1 membrane had the highest selectivity 1.01 × 104 S.s.cm−3. In summary SPS/CND composite membrane exhibit excellent combination of functional properties and offer great promise for its usage in direct methanol fuel cell applications.

Dian Permana: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Investigation. Lukman Atmaja: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Validation, Supervision. Arif Priyangga: Writing – review & editing, Software, Project administration. Yohana Ivana Kedang: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Mardi Santoso: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
